crop is said to be an exceptionally
short one and many of the farmers
have suffered accordingly. Grain
dealers have suffered alike with
the farmers, while the Independent
dealer has profited.


Not much damage has been done
to grain that has remained on the
outside. Mr.. Stevens, an indepen-
dent dealers at Elmira, has $4,000
bushels outside his warehouse, but
it is still in fine condition. A whole
train load of wheat was removed
from Davenport last Thursday. and
stations farther down the Washing
ton Central line must begin to get
relief at once in order that the
warehouses may be cleared in time
for this years harvest.


These conditions have been in
flicted by traffic conditions and all
will be relieved contomporaneously
with the remedying Of railroad con
iestiOn. With wheat moving there
will be plenty of money for the
farmers, as grain dealers will CON
tract all this years yield at 60
cents, the market price at the pres
ent time. which is 10 cents higher
than was paid Jan. |
